design_philosophy.core_loop: Combat-focused platforming with weapon variety and resource management. Players navigate prehistoric levels, defeat enemies using diverse weapons (boomerangs, bones, fire, flints, electricity, stone wheels, clubs), and battle bosses to progress toward rescuing kidnapped women.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time-pressure health decay system during standard levels (health decreases over time) versus boss encounters (health static during boss battles). Two-player mutual damage creates competitive tension.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Caveman action hero wielding prehistoric weaponry to overcome dinosaurs and rival tribesmen in a colorful, comic-styled prehistoric world.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The health-decay mechanic during levels creates constant urgency and discourages camping/passive play. Route selection after bosses (multiple paths to different endings) emphasizes player choice and replayability. Weapon switching as core mechanic rather than single-gun progression. Caveman aesthetic with 'cute' comic touches applied to action-heavy gameplay (noted as 'cutesie jumpy-jumpy game' by reviewers) creates tonal distinctiveness.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players can select different routes at end of boss battles in arcade/Genesis/Amiga versions, affecting subsequent level progression. Two-player mode allows cooperative or competitive difficulty modulation. Final boss defeat offers three exit choices leading to different endings.

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon pickup and switching system. Different weapons available (boomerangs, bones, fire, flints, electricity, stone wheels, clubs) with charging capability in arcade/Genesis versions. SNES version removes charging mechanic and some weapons.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats unlock route selection; final boss defeat unlocks ending choice. Levels progress sequentially in most versions; SNES version features overworld map for level selection with longer level designs and bonus stages.
replayability_hooks: Multiple route selection after boss encounters
Three different ending sequences based on final exit choice
Different weapon loadouts and strategies per playthrough
SNES-exclusive overworld map and bonus stages

world_building_techniques: Prehistoric setting (dinosaurs, caveman aesthetics, rival tribe antagonists)
Comic-styled visual presentation noted by reviewers as 'neat comic touches'
Japanese arcade version included narrative scene of cavemen dragging women by hair (removed from US release for content sensitivity)

boss_design.philosophy: Distinct boss encounters at level conclusions that puncture the time-pressure health-decay mechanic with static-health battles. Boss variety across prehistoric enemies (Tyrannosaurus documented as first-level boss). Boss defeats trigger route selection, making bosses narrative/progression gatekeepers beyond combat challenges.

danger_signals: Multiple weapon types needed (environmental hazards require adaptation)
environmental_storytelling: Prehistoric level design communicates hostile world
Weapon variety scattered through levels reflects survival toolkit
Route branching after bosses suggests controlled wilderness with multiple survival paths

sources_used: Wikipedia: Joe & Mac article (gameplay section, ports section, reception section)
